---
title: "D*"
---

## Overview

D* is a family of incremental replanning algorithms for environments that can change while a robot is moving.

Instead of planning from scratch after each map update, D* updates parts of the previous search result, which can reduce total replanning effort.

D* is not usually faster than A* for a single planning query. Its advantage appears when the environment changes repeatedly and replanning is required many times.

## When to Use It

Use D* when:

- the map is discovered online or changes over time,
- replanning must happen repeatedly,
- and response time matters as much as final path quality.

## Core Idea

A typical D* workflow is:

1. Build an initial plan.
2. Detect map/cost changes (for example, a newly observed obstacle).
3. Repair the plan incrementally instead of restarting from zero.

D* Lite is a widely used simplified formulation of this idea.
The most commonly implemented modern variant is D* Lite, which reformulates the original D* ideas using concepts similar to A* and incremental graph search.

## Why It Matters

In real robots, static assumptions break quickly.
Doors close, people move, and sensors reveal new obstacles.
D* methods help maintain a valid route without full replanning every cycle.

## Trade-Offs

- Strong fit for dynamic maps and repeated updates.
- More complex than single-shot A*.
- Requires careful bookkeeping for consistency and performance.
- Benefits are most visible when updates are frequent.
- If the environment rarely changes, repeated A* replanning may be simpler and perfectly adequate.

## How It Relates to A*

- A* is often used as a one-shot planner on a fixed map.
- D* focuses on efficient plan repair after map changes.
- Conceptually, A* computes a path from scratch, while D* attempts to reuse previous search work after map updates.
- D* can be viewed as an incremental-search extension for dynamic settings.

## Algorithm Sketch

Classic D* is usually presented as an incremental repair process over changing edge costs.

Pseudo-code level view:

1. Compute an initial path using current map costs.
2. Start moving along that path.
3. Detect a new obstacle or changed traversal cost.
4. Mark affected states as needing repair.
5. Propagate cost changes through the previously computed search structure.
6. Recompute only the inconsistent part of the route.
7. Continue moving and repeat when the map changes again.

The central idea is not that D* finds a different style of path than A*, but that it tries to avoid restarting the whole search after every local update.

## Comparison Notebook {#demo}

- [Open In Jupyter Lab](http://localhost:8888/lab/tree/notebooks/path-planning/grid-graph/d_star_lite_demo.ipynb)

Why this link is used here: D* Lite is the cleaner modern formulation to implement in a teaching notebook, and the side-by-side comparison against repeated A* makes the computational reuse easier to see.
